article.title: Characterization of novel regulators for heat stress tolerance in tomato from Indian sub‐continent
article.license: This is an open access article under the terms of the http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/4.0/ License, which permits use, distribution and reproduction in any medium, provided the original work is properly cited.

events.event_label: Acylsugars accumulate in Solanaceae and affect whiteflies and spider mites
events.evidence_context_text: PMC7540533.p026.s001: Solyc09g014280 gene codes for an HXXXD‐type acyltransferase and belongs to the acylsugar acyltransferases (ASATs) class of the large and diverse BAHD family of acyltransferases (Moghe et al.,). PMC7540533.p026.s002: Acylsugars are a group of small specialized metabolites having diverse structures that are restricted to plants in the Solanaceae family that act as natural chemicals against pests like whiteflies and spider mites (Alba et al.,; Liedl et al.,). PMC7540533.p026.s003: Acylsugars are produced in the tip cell of the long glandular secreting trichomes using ASAT enzymes that catalyse sequential addition of specific acyl chains to the sucrose molecule using acyl CoA donors (Maldonado et al.,; Schilmiller et al.,). PMC7540533.p026.s004: Solyc09g014280 and Solyc05g052670 (another tolerant‐up gene but not being functionally characterized; Figure S10) are reported to be enriched in the glandular trichomes in tomato (Moghe et al.,).

events.event_label: ZAT12, NAC019, bZIP28 and PIF4 confer thermotolerance
events.evidence_context_text: PMC7540533.p020.s015: The highly HS‐responsive TF families with more than 20 of its members significantly modulated (up‐regulated and down‐regulated) at transcript level include bHLH, bZIP, C2H2, C3H, ERF, GRAS, HD‐ZIP, HSF, MYB, NAC and WRKY (Figure 3d). PMC7540533.p020.s016: Further, we scanned for those TF families that had at least two differentially regulated members in response to HS and also had ≥80% of its members exhibiting either up‐ or down‐regulation. PMC7540533.p020.s017: Using these criteria, members belonging to TF families B3, C3H, HSF, NAC and Trihelix were majorly induced in response to HS, while ARF, Dof, MADS, TCP and YABBY were primarily repressed in response to HS (Figure 3d). PMC7540533.p020.s018: Thermotolerance in transgenic systems has been achieved by modulating the levels of several TFs like ZAT12 (Shah et al.,), NAC019 (Guan et al.,), bZIP28 (Srivastava et al.,) and PIF4 (a bHLH transcription factor) in Arabidopsis (Gangappa et al.,; Hwang et al.,).

events.event_label: HS occurs in Tomato
events.evidence_context_text: PMC7540533.p006.s009: Till now, the knowledge about the effect of HS on tomato leaf by exploiting the comparative transcriptomics in contrasting cultivars is not well understood. PMC7540533.p006.s010: Tomato is increasingly becoming a cash crop in regions of higher temperatures (the tropics and sub‐tropics) than the optimum 26/20 °C during the photoperiod/dark period (Srivastava et al.,). PMC7540533.p006.s011: One way for developing HS‐resilient crops is to exploit the naturally evolved mechanistic signalling frameworks in cultivars exhibiting contrasting response to HS (Challinor et al.,). PMC7540533.p006.s012: To uncover the HS‐responsive molecular mechanisms in tomato using naturally occurring cultivars growing in the sub‐tropical climatic conditions of the Indian sub‐continent, we first evaluated nine tolerant/sensitive tomato cultivars under HS to identify the best contrasting pair, viz. PMC7540533.p006.s013: CLN1621L (henceforth referred to as CLN, tolerant) and CA4 (sensitive).

events.event_label: ASAT catalyzes sucrose modification using acyl CoA to produce acylsugar
events.evidence_context_text: PMC7540533.p026.s001: Solyc09g014280 gene codes for an HXXXD‐type acyltransferase and belongs to the acylsugar acyltransferases (ASATs) class of the large and diverse BAHD family of acyltransferases (Moghe et al.,). PMC7540533.p026.s002: Acylsugars are a group of small specialized metabolites having diverse structures that are restricted to plants in the Solanaceae family that act as natural chemicals against pests like whiteflies and spider mites (Alba et al.,; Liedl et al.,). PMC7540533.p026.s003: Acylsugars are produced in the tip cell of the long glandular secreting trichomes using ASAT enzymes that catalyse sequential addition of specific acyl chains to the sucrose molecule using acyl CoA donors (Maldonado et al.,; Schilmiller et al.,). PMC7540533.p026.s004: Solyc09g014280 and Solyc05g052670 (another tolerant‐up gene but not being functionally characterized; Figure S10) are reported to be enriched in the glandular trichomes in tomato (Moghe et al.,).

events.event_label: PI‑II inhibits trypsin and chymotrypsin and contributes to plant defence
events.evidence_context_text: PMC7540533.p031.s001: Furthermore, another gene but a ‘sensitive‐up gene’ which is Solyc03g020030 was functionally characterized. PMC7540533.p031.s002: This belongs to the proteinase inhibitor‐II (PI‐II) serine‐PI family having dimeric domains in the protein, inhibiting trypsin and chymotrypsin (Tamhane et al.,). PMC7540533.p031.s003: The PI‐IIs are well documented in plant defence against insects, bacteria and fungi (Rehman et al.,). PMC7540533.p031.s004: Few reports in abiotic stress tolerance like drought, salinity, osmotic variations and pH are also documented (Huang et al.,; Shan et al.,; Srinivasan et al.,).

events.event_label: HS up‑regulates HSF family members A1b, A2, A3, A4c, A5, A7, C1 and HSFA2
events.evidence_context_text: PMC7540533.p022.s001: The most studied and well‐established TF family in response to HS is the HSF family. PMC7540533.p022.s002: Seven tomato HSFs (A1b, A2, A3, A4c, A5, A7 and C1) exhibited HS‐mediated up‐regulation in both the cultivars with highest up‐regulation for HSFA2 (Table S4). PMC7540533.p022.s003: We noted that the repressor class B HSF members, namely HSFB1, B2a and B2b, are up‐regulated only in the sensitive CA4. PMC7540533.p022.s004: Whether this has any direct correlation with CA4 being HS sensitive needs to be further evaluated.

events.event_label: HS modulates expression of multiple TF families
events.evidence_context_text: PMC7540533.p020.s012: Moreover, 26 TFs showed HS‐mediated strong repression (FC ≤ 5) in both cultivars (Figure 3c). PMC7540533.p020.s013: While Solyc08g008280 (WRKY‐53) was the top repressed candidate in CLN, Solyc10g005080 (late elongated hypocotyl) was highly down‐regulated TF in CA4 under HS (Figure 3c). PMC7540533.p020.s014: In addition, other TFs exhibiting strong repression belong to TF families bHLH (4), bZIP (2), C2H2 (3), MYB (3), TCP (2), WRKY (2) and YABBY (2) (Figure 3c and Table S4). PMC7540533.p020.s015: The highly HS‐responsive TF families with more than 20 of its members significantly modulated (up‐regulated and down‐regulated) at transcript level include bHLH, bZIP, C2H2, C3H, ERF, GRAS, HD‐ZIP, HSF, MYB, NAC and WRKY (Figure 3d). PMC7540533.p020.s016: Further, we scanned for those TF families that had at least two differentially regulated members in response to HS and also had ≥80% of its members exhibiting either up‐ or down‐regulation.

events.event_label: HS modulates expression of multiple ERFs
events.evidence_context_text: PMC7540533.p022.s009: Furthermore, we investigated the ERF family that showed highest number of HS‐responsive TFs and include AP2, DREBs and ERFs (Sakuma et al.,). PMC7540533.p022.s010: Various ERFs are reported to mediate development and stress response, but only few address their regulatory role in thermotolerance (Licausi et al.,; Klay et al.,; Müller and Munné‐Bosch, ). PMC7540533.p022.s011: Out of 147 ERFs reported in tomato, 30 members followed differential HS response in CLN and CA4 (Table S4). PMC7540533.p022.s012: Of these, 12 ERFs were similarly regulated (9 up‐regulated and 3‐regulated down) in both cultivars, while others were regulated in cultivar‐biased manner. PMC7540533.p022.s013: We selected three previously uncharacterized ERFs (ERF.F4, ERF.F5, and ERF.C1) in HS response based on the observation that they all maintained high levels in CLN and low levels in CA4 under HS.

events.event_label: Solyc09g014280 and Solyc05g052670 are expressed in glandular trichomes
events.evidence_context_text: PMC7540533.p026.s003: Acylsugars are produced in the tip cell of the long glandular secreting trichomes using ASAT enzymes that catalyse sequential addition of specific acyl chains to the sucrose molecule using acyl CoA donors (Maldonado et al.,; Schilmiller et al.,). PMC7540533.p026.s004: Solyc09g014280 and Solyc05g052670 (another tolerant‐up gene but not being functionally characterized; Figure S10) are reported to be enriched in the glandular trichomes in tomato (Moghe et al.,). PMC7540533.p026.s005: While Solyc09g014280 is induced upon potato cyst nematode infection in tomato roots (Swiecicka et al.,), Solyc05g052670 is up‐regulated in tomato in response to tomato yellow leaf curl virus infection (Chen et al.,). PMC7540533.p026.s006: Here, we report yet unexplored function of an ASAT member in tomato HS response. PMC7540533.p026.s007: Plants exhibiting successful knock‐down of Solyc09g014280 transcripts (Figure S11) showed severe drooping of leaves upon recovery after being exposed to HS in comparison with vector control plants (Figure 4a).

events.event_label: HS induces expression of B3, C3H, HSF, NAC and Trihelix TF families
events.evidence_context_text: PMC7540533.p020.s015: The highly HS‐responsive TF families with more than 20 of its members significantly modulated (up‐regulated and down‐regulated) at transcript level include bHLH, bZIP, C2H2, C3H, ERF, GRAS, HD‐ZIP, HSF, MYB, NAC and WRKY (Figure 3d). PMC7540533.p020.s016: Further, we scanned for those TF families that had at least two differentially regulated members in response to HS and also had ≥80% of its members exhibiting either up‐ or down‐regulation. PMC7540533.p020.s017: Using these criteria, members belonging to TF families B3, C3H, HSF, NAC and Trihelix were majorly induced in response to HS, while ARF, Dof, MADS, TCP and YABBY were primarily repressed in response to HS (Figure 3d). PMC7540533.p020.s018: Thermotolerance in transgenic systems has been achieved by modulating the levels of several TFs like ZAT12 (Shah et al.,), NAC019 (Guan et al.,), bZIP28 (Srivastava et al.,) and PIF4 (a bHLH transcription factor) in Arabidopsis (Gangappa et al.,; Hwang et al.,).

events.event_label: HS up‑regulates HSFA6a and down‑regulates HSFA4b
events.evidence_context_text: PMC7540533.p022.s006: The Arabidopsis hsfb1‐hsfb2b double knockout mutant demonstrates higher acquired thermotolerance (Ikeda et al.,; Kumar et al.,). PMC7540533.p022.s007: HSFA6a was up‐regulated, and HSFA4b was down‐regulated under HS only in CLN but not in CA4 (Table S4). PMC7540533.p022.s008: AtHSFA6a is induced in response to ABA, salt and drought and is responsible for the transcriptional activation of stress‐responsive gene DREB2a via ABA‐dependent signalling pathway (Hwang et al.,). PMC7540533.p022.s009: Furthermore, we investigated the ERF family that showed highest number of HS‐responsive TFs and include AP2, DREBs and ERFs (Sakuma et al.,). PMC7540533.p022.s010: Various ERFs are reported to mediate development and stress response, but only few address their regulatory role in thermotolerance (Licausi et al.,; Klay et al.,; Müller and Munné‐Bosch, ).

events.event_label: HS up‑regulates ERF.F4, ERF.F5 and ERF.C1
events.evidence_context_text: PMC7540533.p022.s012: Of these, 12 ERFs were similarly regulated (9 up‐regulated and 3‐regulated down) in both cultivars, while others were regulated in cultivar‐biased manner. PMC7540533.p022.s013: We selected three previously uncharacterized ERFs (ERF.F4, ERF.F5, and ERF.C1) in HS response based on the observation that they all maintained high levels in CLN and low levels in CA4 under HS. PMC7540533.p022.s014: This was validated by qRT‐PCR also (Figure 3e). PMC7540533.p022.s015: These 3 ERFs are reported in fruit development (Hao et al.,; Di Matteo et al.,) in tomato, but their role in thermotolerance was lacking. PMC7540533.p022.s016: Functional characterization of the above three ERFs by transient overexpression significantly enhanced (more than doubled) the tomato seedling survival rate under HS as compared to vector control plants (Figure 3f) with highest survival percentage for ERF.F5.

events.event_label: Acylsugar acyltransferase and Notabilis enhance HS tolerance
events.evidence_context_text: PMC7540533.p003.s003: Comparative transcriptome‐wide signatures of a tolerant (CLN1621L) and sensitive (CA4) cultivar pair shortlisted from a pool of varieties exhibiting variable thermo‐sensitivity using physiological‐, survival‐ and yield‐related traits revealed redundant to cultivar‐specific HS regulation. PMC7540533.p003.s004: The antagonistically expressing genes encode enzymes and proteins that have roles in plant defence and abiotic stresses. PMC7540533.p003.s005: Functional characterization of three antagonistic genes by overexpression and silencing established Solyc09g014280 (Acylsugar acyltransferase) and Solyc07g056570 (Notabilis) that are up‐regulated in tolerant cultivar, as positive regulators of HS tolerance and Solyc03g020030 (Pin‐II proteinase inhibitor), that are down‐regulated in CLN1621L, as negative regulator of thermotolerance. PMC7540533.p003.s006: Transcriptional assessment of promoters of these genes by SNPs in stress‐responsive cis‐elements and promoter swapping experiments in opposite cultivar background showed inherent cultivar‐specific orchestration of transcription factors in regulating transcription. PMC7540533.p003.s007: Moreover, overexpression of three ethylene response transcription factors (ERF.C1/F4/F5) also improved HS tolerance in tomato.

events.event_label: HSP21 protects PSII and contributes to fruit ripening
events.evidence_context_text: PMC7540533.p006.s003: Transcript profiling using cDNA‐AFLP (Bita et al.,) and microarray analysis (Frank et al.,) as well as proteomic analyses (Mazzeo et al.,) of microspores has shown active involvement of HSPs, ROS scavengers, hormones, amino acid metabolism and nitrogen assimilation in tomato HS response. PMC7540533.p006.s004: Heat‐inducible tomato HSP21 protects PSII from the HS‐induced oxidative stress and also plays role in fruit ripening (Neta‐Sharir et al.,). PMC7540533.p006.s005: Tomato HSFA2 is highly up‐regulated during HS that in turn forms a hetero‐oligomeric transcriptional complex with HSFA1. PMC7540533.p006.s006: In addition, HSP17.4‐CII acts as a co‐repressor of HSFA2 (Port et al.,). PMC7540533.p006.s007: Overexpression of Arabidopsis receptor‐like kinase ERECTA (ER) leads to enhanced HS tolerance in Arabidopsis, rice and tomato with increased biomass (Shen et al.,).

events.event_label: HS increases HSFA2 expression which interacts with HSFA1
events.evidence_context_text: PMC7540533.p006.s003: Transcript profiling using cDNA‐AFLP (Bita et al.,) and microarray analysis (Frank et al.,) as well as proteomic analyses (Mazzeo et al.,) of microspores has shown active involvement of HSPs, ROS scavengers, hormones, amino acid metabolism and nitrogen assimilation in tomato HS response. PMC7540533.p006.s004: Heat‐inducible tomato HSP21 protects PSII from the HS‐induced oxidative stress and also plays role in fruit ripening (Neta‐Sharir et al.,). PMC7540533.p006.s005: Tomato HSFA2 is highly up‐regulated during HS that in turn forms a hetero‐oligomeric transcriptional complex with HSFA1. PMC7540533.p006.s006: In addition, HSP17.4‐CII acts as a co‐repressor of HSFA2 (Port et al.,). PMC7540533.p006.s007: Overexpression of Arabidopsis receptor‐like kinase ERECTA (ER) leads to enhanced HS tolerance in Arabidopsis, rice and tomato with increased biomass (Shen et al.,).

events.event_label: HS changes expression of glycosyltransferase and Marmande
events.evidence_context_text: PMC7540533.p014.s006: Surprisingly, only 21 genes showed an opposite (antagonistic) HS regulation between CLN and CA4 (Figure 2a,b). PMC7540533.p014.s007: Five genes, viz. Solyc03g020030 (PIN‐type‐II proteinase inhibitor 69), Solyc09g084450 (proteinase inhibitor I), Solyc12g013700 (Stem‐specific protein tsjt1), Solyc01g079530 (RING/FYVE/PHD zinc finger superfamily protein) and Solyc12g042500 (gibberellin‐regulated family protein), were down‐regulated in CLN but were up‐regulated in CA4. PMC7540533.p014.s008: There were 16 genes, viz. PMC7540533.p014.s009: Solyc01g065530 (protein COBRA), Solyc01g081250 (glutathione s‐transferase), Solyc01g087020 (transmembrane protein), Solyc01g095140 (late embryogenesis abundant protein), Solyc01g107780 (glycosyltransferase), Solyc03g116890 (WRKY transcription factor 39), Soly05g052670, Solyc05g052680 and Solyc09g014280 (all three HXXXD‐type acyltransferase family proteins), Solyc06g049070 (nucleotide/sugar transporter family protein), Solyc07g008103 (blue copper protein), Solyc07g056570 (Notabilis), Solyc07g064410 (Kua‐ubiquitin conjugating enzyme hybrid), Solyc09g075920 (serine/threonine‐protein kinase), Solyc09g092500 (glycosyltransferase) and Solyc10g081570 (Marmande), were up‐regulated in CLN but down‐regulated in CA4 upon HS. PMC7540533.p014.s010: Gene ontology (GO) enrichment of these antagonistic genes highlighted the significant enrichment of negative regulation of endopeptidase, peptidase and proteolysis, response to water, pollination and transferase activity (Figure 2c).

events.event_label: ASAT and Notabilis affect hypocotyl length
events.evidence_context_text: PMC7540533.p027.s006: Survival was gauged 6 days postrecovery. PMC7540533.p027.s007: (b) DAB and Trypan blue staining of leaves of HS‐treated empty vector (TRV), TRV‐ASAT and TRV‐Notabilis‐silenced plants. PMC7540533.p027.s008: (c–f) Estimation of net photosynthesis rate (μmol/m2/s), water‐use efficiency (mmol/mol), transpiration rate (mmol/m2/s) and stomatal conductance (mol/m2/s) in empty vector (TRV), TRV‐ASAT and TRV‐Notabilis‐silenced plants following heat stress. PMC7540533.p027.s009: (g,h) Estimation of survival (percentage) and percentage reduction in hypocotyl length of seedlings under control conditions and after 5 days of heat stress in plants with overexpression of empty vector, ASAT and Notabilis seedlings. PMC7540533.p027.s010: Data are means of four biological sets of 70 seedlings each. (i) Expression profiles of HSR genes in heat, ABA and VIGS silenced ASAT and Notabilis plants by qRT‐PCR.

events.event_label: Notabilis silencing increases ROS and cell death
events.evidence_context_text: PMC7540533.p029.s009: Reduced thermotolerance was also reported in Arabidopsis mutants with impaired NCED6 and NCED9 expression (Gabriele et al.,). PMC7540533.p029.s010: It is known that high ABA levels cause ROS outburst. PMC7540533.p029.s011: However, the leaves of Notabilis‐silenced plants show slightly increased ROS levels and HS‐induced cell death in comparison with vector control plants (Figure 4b). PMC7540533.p029.s012: This apparent anomaly may be attributed to a balance between low ROS levels due to reduced ABA levels and other pathways contributing to ROS production in response to HS. PMC7540533.p029.s013: The gas exchange analyses further supported the important role of Notabilis in thermotolerance.

PMC7540533.p031.s003: The PI‐IIs are well documented in plant defence against insects, bacteria and fungi (Rehman et al.,). events.event_label: PI‑II knock‑down enhances WUEi and net photosynthetic rates
events.evidence_context_text: PMC7540533.p031.s009: Moreover, there was appreciable difference phenotypically when VIGS was repeated in sensitive background (Pusa Ruby) (Figure 5b). PMC7540533.p031.s010: The CLN PI‐II knock‐down plants exhibited significant enhancement in WUEi and net photosynthetic rates as compared to TRV‐control plants under HS (Figure 5c). PMC7540533.p031.s011: In addition, both the transpiration rate and the stomatal conductance were lower in TRV‐PI‐II plants under HS (Figure 5c,d). PMC7540533.p031.s012: The PI‐II overexpression in CLN decreased the tolerance of the seedlings of this robust cultivar as evident from reduced hypocotyl length as well as survival rate under HS in comparison with vector control seedlings. PMC7540533.p031.s013: In contrast, VIGS‐based silencing of PI‐II in Pusa Ruby exhibited higher survival rate (Figure 5e–g).

events.event_label: PI‑II overexpression reduces hypocotyl length and survival rate
events.evidence_context_text: PMC7540533.p031.s009: Moreover, there was appreciable difference phenotypically when VIGS was repeated in sensitive background (Pusa Ruby) (Figure 5b). PMC7540533.p031.s010: The CLN PI‐II knock‐down plants exhibited significant enhancement in WUEi and net photosynthetic rates as compared to TRV‐control plants under HS (Figure 5c). PMC7540533.p031.s011: In addition, both the transpiration rate and the stomatal conductance were lower in TRV‐PI‐II plants under HS (Figure 5c,d). PMC7540533.p031.s012: The PI‐II overexpression in CLN decreased the tolerance of the seedlings of this robust cultivar as evident from reduced hypocotyl length as well as survival rate under HS in comparison with vector control seedlings. PMC7540533.p031.s013: In contrast, VIGS‐based silencing of PI‐II in Pusa Ruby exhibited higher survival rate (Figure 5e–g).

events.event_label: HS affects phenological development, photosynthesis, and respiration
events.evidence_context_text: PMC7540533.p005.s001: Global warming is exposing plants to unfavourable temperature fluctuations resulting in detrimental effects on crop productivity and an ultimate threat to biodiversity and food security (Battisti and Naylor,; Challinor et al.,). PMC7540533.p005.s002: As per the 5th report of Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change (IPCC), 0.8–4.8 °C rise in the global mean temperatures has been projected by the end of this century (IPCC AR5, Pachauri et al., ). PMC7540533.p005.s003: High temperature affects the phenological development, photosynthesis, respiration, initiation, expansion and senescence of plant organs (Wang et al.,). PMC7540533.p005.s004: As sessile life forms, it is imperative for plants to evolve mechanisms to adapt to the thermal stress to minimize damage on their growth and reproduction.

events.event_label: HSFA1 regulates HSFBs which repress HSFA1 and affect HSR
events.evidence_context_text: PMC7540533.p005.s009: These transcriptional regulators further control the expression of several HS‐inducible genes including HSFA3, nuclear factor‐Y (NF‐Y) and HSPs (Chen et al.,; Sato et al.,; Schramm et al.,; Yoshida et al.,). PMC7540533.p005.s010: In Arabidopsis, HSFA1 regulates HSFBs that in turn finely regulate the HSR by repressing the activity of HSFA1s in a negative feedback loop (Ikeda et al.,). PMC7540533.p005.s011: Another critical regulator of thermotolerance is the C‐REPEAT BINDING FACTOR2 (RCF2)‐regulated NAC019 that acts as an upstream regulator of HSFA1b, HSFA6b, HSFA7a and HSFC1 (Guan et al.,). PMC7540533.p005.s012: Several HSF‐target modules like HSFA4a‐APX1 and HSFA5‐HSPs act independent of HSFA1 pathway (Liu et al.,; Von Koskull‐Doring et al.,). PMC7540533.p005.s013: In addition, a flexible mode of heat response pathways involving bZIP28‐, HSFA2‐ and ROS‐dependent signals is also demonstrated in Arabidopsis (Kataoka et al.,).

events.event_label: bZIP28, HSFA2 and ROS participate in heat response pathways
events.evidence_context_text: PMC7540533.p005.s012: Several HSF‐target modules like HSFA4a‐APX1 and HSFA5‐HSPs act independent of HSFA1 pathway (Liu et al.,; Von Koskull‐Doring et al.,). PMC7540533.p005.s013: In addition, a flexible mode of heat response pathways involving bZIP28‐, HSFA2‐ and ROS‐dependent signals is also demonstrated in Arabidopsis (Kataoka et al.,). PMC7540533.p005.s014: Apart from these canonical HSR circuits, PIF4 regulatory and associated signalling networks control plant thermo‐morphogenesis (Koini et al.,; Oh et al.,; Proveniers and Van Zanten,; Stavang et al.,). PMC7540533.p005.s015: The significance of post‐translational regulation in HS response is also well documented in plant HSR (Agarwal et al.,; Hahn et al.,; Miller et al.,; Mizoi et al.,; Sato et al.,; Vainonen et al.,). PMC7540533.p005.s016: Evidences also suggest orchestration of multi‐layered regulatory systems comprising not only TFs but also epigenetic regulators and small RNAs as important HSR players.

events.event_label: ASAT suppression increases hydrogen peroxide and cell death
events.evidence_context_text: PMC7540533.p026.s009: These parameters are routinely adopted to assess thermotolerance of plants. PMC7540533.p026.s010: The suppression of ASAT gene shows enhanced hydrogen peroxide (one of several reactive oxygen species) levels as well as cell death under HS as judged by DAB staining and Trypan blue staining, respectively (Figure 4b). PMC7540533.p026.s011: Gas exchange parameters measured by LI‐COR 6400 portable photosynthesis measuring system at the end of the heat treatment of ASAT‐silenced plants showed significant drop in net photosynthesis rate and water‐use efficiency (WUEi) as well as enhanced stomatal conductance and transpiration rate (Figure 4c–f) highlighting the role of ASAT as a positive regulator of thermotolerance. PMC7540533.p026.s012: This conclusion was further strengthened by assessing seedling performance under HS when ASAT gene was transiently overexpressed. PMC7540533.p026.s013: The seedling survival increased by nearly 17% in comparison with vector control (Figure 4g), and the reduction in hypocotyl length under HS was also around 8% in overexpression plants in comparison with 18% in vector control plants (Figure 4h).

events.event_label: TaNCED overexpression confers drought tolerance and reduces stomatal conductance and transpiration
events.evidence_context_text: PMC7540533.p029.s012: This apparent anomaly may be attributed to a balance between low ROS levels due to reduced ABA levels and other pathways contributing to ROS production in response to HS. PMC7540533.p029.s013: The gas exchange analyses further supported the important role of Notabilis in thermotolerance. PMC7540533.p029.s014: There was a significant reduction in the photosynthetic rate and water‐use efficiency along with a significant rise in stomatal conductance and enhanced transpiration rate in silenced plants in contrast to vector control plants (Figure 4c–f). PMC7540533.p029.s015: Overexpression of wheat TaNCED gene in Arabidopsis enhanced tolerance to drought stress by reducing stomatal conductance and transpiration (Tong et al.,). PMC7540533.p029.s016: Further proof for Notabilis as a gene imparting thermotolerance was provided from tomato plants overexpressing Notabilis as judged by better seedling survival percentage and hypocotyl length in comparison with vector control (Figure 4g,h).

events.event_label: HSFA1s relocates to nucleus and activates HSFA7s, HSFA2, DREB2A and MBF1C
events.evidence_context_text: PMC7540533.p005.s006: It is therefore necessary to have in‐depth understanding of the plant thermotolerance mechanisms at molecular level to achieve sustainable food production. PMC7540533.p005.s007: The canonical HSR involves HS sensing by Phytochrome B (Jung et al.,), the heat‐induced modulations at the level of membrane stability activating the calcium and lipid signalling (Saidi et al.,), and the interplay of conserved heat stress transcription factor‐heat shock protein (HSF‐HSP) module. PMC7540533.p005.s008: Upon HS, HSFA1s, the master regulators of HSR, are relieved from repression by HSP70/90, relocate from cytoplasm to nucleus and activate other HSFs (HSFA7s, HSFA2 and HSFBs), dehydration‐responsive element‐binding protein 2A (DREB2A) and multiprotein bridging factor 1C (MBF1C) (Liu et al.,; Mishra et al.,; Yoshida et al.,). PMC7540533.p005.s009: These transcriptional regulators further control the expression of several HS‐inducible genes including HSFA3, nuclear factor‐Y (NF‐Y) and HSPs (Chen et al.,; Sato et al.,; Schramm et al.,; Yoshida et al.,). PMC7540533.p005.s010: In Arabidopsis, HSFA1 regulates HSFBs that in turn finely regulate the HSR by repressing the activity of HSFA1s in a negative feedback loop (Ikeda et al.,).

events.event_label: NAC019 contributes to thermotolerance and regulates HSFA1b, HSFA6b, HSFA7a and HSFC1
events.evidence_context_text: PMC7540533.p005.s009: These transcriptional regulators further control the expression of several HS‐inducible genes including HSFA3, nuclear factor‐Y (NF‐Y) and HSPs (Chen et al.,; Sato et al.,; Schramm et al.,; Yoshida et al.,). PMC7540533.p005.s010: In Arabidopsis, HSFA1 regulates HSFBs that in turn finely regulate the HSR by repressing the activity of HSFA1s in a negative feedback loop (Ikeda et al.,). PMC7540533.p005.s011: Another critical regulator of thermotolerance is the C‐REPEAT BINDING FACTOR2 (RCF2)‐regulated NAC019 that acts as an upstream regulator of HSFA1b, HSFA6b, HSFA7a and HSFC1 (Guan et al.,). PMC7540533.p005.s012: Several HSF‐target modules like HSFA4a‐APX1 and HSFA5‐HSPs act independent of HSFA1 pathway (Liu et al.,; Von Koskull‐Doring et al.,). PMC7540533.p005.s013: In addition, a flexible mode of heat response pathways involving bZIP28‐, HSFA2‐ and ROS‐dependent signals is also demonstrated in Arabidopsis (Kataoka et al.,).

events.event_label: ASAT gene modulation alters ROS, cell death and improves thermotolerance and seedling performance
events.evidence_context_text: PMC7540533.p026.s009: These parameters are routinely adopted to assess thermotolerance of plants. PMC7540533.p026.s010: The suppression of ASAT gene shows enhanced hydrogen peroxide (one of several reactive oxygen species) levels as well as cell death under HS as judged by DAB staining and Trypan blue staining, respectively (Figure 4b). PMC7540533.p026.s011: Gas exchange parameters measured by LI‐COR 6400 portable photosynthesis measuring system at the end of the heat treatment of ASAT‐silenced plants showed significant drop in net photosynthesis rate and water‐use efficiency (WUEi) as well as enhanced stomatal conductance and transpiration rate (Figure 4c–f) highlighting the role of ASAT as a positive regulator of thermotolerance. PMC7540533.p026.s012: This conclusion was further strengthened by assessing seedling performance under HS when ASAT gene was transiently overexpressed. PMC7540533.p026.s013: The seedling survival increased by nearly 17% in comparison with vector control (Figure 4g), and the reduction in hypocotyl length under HS was also around 8% in overexpression plants in comparison with 18% in vector control plants (Figure 4h).

events.event_label: HS changes expression of multiple genes (protein COBRA, GST, transmembrane protein, LEA protein, glycosyltransferase, WRKY39, HXXXD acyltransferases, transporter, blue copper protein, Notabilis)
events.evidence_context_text: PMC7540533.p014.s006: Surprisingly, only 21 genes showed an opposite (antagonistic) HS regulation between CLN and CA4 (Figure 2a,b). PMC7540533.p014.s007: Five genes, viz. Solyc03g020030 (PIN‐type‐II proteinase inhibitor 69), Solyc09g084450 (proteinase inhibitor I), Solyc12g013700 (Stem‐specific protein tsjt1), Solyc01g079530 (RING/FYVE/PHD zinc finger superfamily protein) and Solyc12g042500 (gibberellin‐regulated family protein), were down‐regulated in CLN but were up‐regulated in CA4. PMC7540533.p014.s008: There were 16 genes, viz. PMC7540533.p014.s009: Solyc01g065530 (protein COBRA), Solyc01g081250 (glutathione s‐transferase), Solyc01g087020 (transmembrane protein), Solyc01g095140 (late embryogenesis abundant protein), Solyc01g107780 (glycosyltransferase), Solyc03g116890 (WRKY transcription factor 39), Soly05g052670, Solyc05g052680 and Solyc09g014280 (all three HXXXD‐type acyltransferase family proteins), Solyc06g049070 (nucleotide/sugar transporter family protein), Solyc07g008103 (blue copper protein), Solyc07g056570 (Notabilis), Solyc07g064410 (Kua‐ubiquitin conjugating enzyme hybrid), Solyc09g075920 (serine/threonine‐protein kinase), Solyc09g092500 (glycosyltransferase) and Solyc10g081570 (Marmande), were up‐regulated in CLN but down‐regulated in CA4 upon HS. PMC7540533.p014.s010: Gene ontology (GO) enrichment of these antagonistic genes highlighted the significant enrichment of negative regulation of endopeptidase, peptidase and proteolysis, response to water, pollination and transferase activity (Figure 2c).

events.event_label: ZAT12, NAC019, bZIP28 and PIF4 confer thermotolerance
events.evidence_context_text: PMC7540533.p020.s015: The highly HS‐responsive TF families with more than 20 of its members significantly modulated (up‐regulated and down‐regulated) at transcript level include bHLH, bZIP, C2H2, C3H, ERF, GRAS, HD‐ZIP, HSF, MYB, NAC and WRKY (Figure 3d). PMC7540533.p020.s016: Further, we scanned for those TF families that had at least two differentially regulated members in response to HS and also had ≥80% of its members exhibiting either up‐ or down‐regulation. PMC7540533.p020.s017: Using these criteria, members belonging to TF families B3, C3H, HSF, NAC and Trihelix were majorly induced in response to HS, while ARF, Dof, MADS, TCP and YABBY were primarily repressed in response to HS (Figure 3d). PMC7540533.p020.s018: Thermotolerance in transgenic systems has been achieved by modulating the levels of several TFs like ZAT12 (Shah et al.,), NAC019 (Guan et al.,), bZIP28 (Srivastava et al.,) and PIF4 (a bHLH transcription factor) in Arabidopsis (Gangappa et al.,; Hwang et al.,).

events.event_label: PI‑II knock‑down enhances WUEi and photosynthesis; overexpression reduces hypocotyl length and survival under HS
events.evidence_context_text: PMC7540533.p031.s009: Moreover, there was appreciable difference phenotypically when VIGS was repeated in sensitive background (Pusa Ruby) (Figure 5b). PMC7540533.p031.s010: The CLN PI‐II knock‐down plants exhibited significant enhancement in WUEi and net photosynthetic rates as compared to TRV‐control plants under HS (Figure 5c). PMC7540533.p031.s011: In addition, both the transpiration rate and the stomatal conductance were lower in TRV‐PI‐II plants under HS (Figure 5c,d). PMC7540533.p031.s012: The PI‐II overexpression in CLN decreased the tolerance of the seedlings of this robust cultivar as evident from reduced hypocotyl length as well as survival rate under HS in comparison with vector control seedlings. PMC7540533.p031.s013: In contrast, VIGS‐based silencing of PI‐II in Pusa Ruby exhibited higher survival rate (Figure 5e–g).

events.event_label: HS response is associated with phosphate starvation
events.evidence_context_text: PMC7540533.p016.s001: Around ~49% (3402 out of 6954) HS‐responsive genes followed conserved HS‐regulated gene expression; 1752 DEGs were up‐regulated and 1650 DEGs were down‐regulated in both cultivars (Figure 2a), signifying a core universal HS response. PMC7540533.p016.s002: GO enrichment analysis suggested the involvement of up‐regulated genes in cellular response to nutrient starvation specifically phosphate starvation in addition to protein folding (Figure S3a,b). PMC7540533.p016.s003: Furthermore, proteins belonging to chaperones, winged helix TFs, mRNA splicing and processing factors were significantly enriched among the conserved up‐regulated genes (Figure S3c). PMC7540533.p016.s004: KEGG pathways like protein processing in ER, spliceosome and ubiquitin‐mediated proteolysis were enriched in conserved up‐regulated genes (Table S3).

events.event_label: ASAT overexpression mitigates HS‑induced hypocotyl shortening and confers thermotolerance
events.evidence_context_text: PMC7540533.p026.s012: This conclusion was further strengthened by assessing seedling performance under HS when ASAT gene was transiently overexpressed. PMC7540533.p026.s013: The seedling survival increased by nearly 17% in comparison with vector control (Figure 4g), and the reduction in hypocotyl length under HS was also around 8% in overexpression plants in comparison with 18% in vector control plants (Figure 4h). PMC7540533.p026.s014: Moreover, the relative abundance of HS marker genes (HSFA2a/A7a/A3a, sHSP24.5 CI and HSP90) (Figure 4i) was reduced several folds in ASAT‐silenced plants reiterating the fact that ASAT has a key role in providing thermotolerance. PMC7540533.p026.s015: Further, to understand how the transcriptional regulation of ASAT results in antagonistic expression between the contrasting cultivars, we sequenced 1.5 kb promoter from both the cultivars and checked promoter ASAT:GUS–reporter expression by assaying CLN promoter:GUS–reporter in CA4 background and vice versa. PMC7540533.p026.s016: We found that there was one substitution of ‘T’ to ‘A’ in CA4 promoter, which disrupted the binding sites of two TFs, namely NF‐Y A/B/C and AP2 having cis‐binding sites, that is ACAAT and AATCAA, respectively, in CA4.

events.evidence_context_text: PMC7540533.p031.s015: In tomato, SlbZIP1 TF (Solyc01g079480.3) transcriptionally regulates PI‐II (Zhu et al.,). PMC7540533.p031.s016: When we checked our NGS data for a possible cultivar‐specific differential response of PI‐II by SlbZIP1 in response to HS, the TF was not significantly differentially regulated in CLN (1.85‐fold change) and CA4 (1.25‐fold change) (Table S2). PMC7540533.p031.s017: This suggested that HS‐mediated regulation of PI‐II is governed by other TFs or may have epigenetic regulation. PMC7540533.p031.s018: The down‐regulation of PI‐II by VIGS established its role in retrograde signalling in the regulation of HS. PMC7540533.p031.s019: Knocking down this gene by CRISPR/RNAi technology could be highly promising for engineering for HS‐thermotolerance.

events.evidence_context_text: PMC7540533.p017.s003: To further discriminate the true cultivar‐biased HS‐responsive DEGs, the above genes were filtered out by adopting strict parameters (See Materials and Methods). PMC7540533.p017.s005: Genes exhibiting the CLN‐specific down‐regulation were enriched in terms associated with ‘photosynthesis’ in biological process and cellular component categories, while glycopeptide alpha‐N‐acetylgalactosaminidase activity, cysteine synthase activity, chlorophyll binding, and ATPase activity were enriched among the molecular function category (Figure S7). PMC7540533.p017.s006: For genes exhibiting the CLN‐specific up‐regulation, there was no significant enrichment, while there was significant enrichment of translation, ribosomal proteins, ribosome, structural constituents of ribosomes, etc., in CA4‐specific up‐regulated genes (Figure S8). events.event_label: Notabilis knock‑down reduces thermotolerance and increases cell death
events.evidence_context_text: PMC7540533.p029.s006: Here, we investigate the role of Notabilis in tomato thermotolerance. PMC7540533.p029.s007: When Notabilis knocked‐down plants (Figure S11) were exposed to HS and assessed after recovery, they exhibited decreased thermotolerance as evidenced by the morphological (severe wilting of leaves) phenotype (Figure 4a). PMC7540533.p029.s008: This is in accordance with the phenotype observed in the tomato null mutant of notabilis that is deficient in ABA synthesis (Burbidge et al.,; Thompson et al.,). PMC7540533.p029.s009: Reduced thermotolerance was also reported in Arabidopsis mutants with impaired NCED6 and NCED9 expression (Gabriele et al.,). PMC7540533.p029.s010: It is known that high ABA levels cause ROS outburst. PMC7540533.p029.s011: However, the leaves of Notabilis‐silenced plants show slightly increased ROS levels and HS‐induced cell death in comparison with vector control plants (Figure 4b). PMC7540533.p029.s012: This apparent anomaly may be attributed to a balance between low ROS levels due to reduced ABA levels and other pathways contributing to ROS production in response to HS. PMC7540533.p029.s013: The gas exchange analyses further supported the important role of Notabilis in thermotolerance.

events.evidence_context_text: PMC7540533.p029.s001: We then functionally validated the other tolerant‐up gene, Solyc07g056570 or Notabilis (9‐cis‐epoxycarotenoid dioxygenase 1). PMC7540533.p029.s002: Notabilis regulates the rate‐limiting step in the ABA biosynthesis pathway and has important role in water stress tolerance as evidenced by several reports (Burbidge et al.,; He et al.,; Luchi et al.,; Qin and Zeevaart,; Wan and Li,). PMC7540533.p029.s003: In Arabidopsis, ABA‐deficient and signalling mutants are involved in acquired thermotolerance acquisition (Larkindale and Huang,). PMC7540533.p029.s004: Arabidopsis thaliana NGATHA1 induces ABA biosynthesis by activating Notabilis homolog NCED3 gene during dehydration stress (Sato et al.,). PMC7540533.p029.s006: Here, we investigate the role of Notabilis in tomato thermotolerance. PMC7540533.p029.s007: When Notabilis knocked‐down plants (Figure S11) were exposed to HS and assessed after recovery, they exhibited decreased thermotolerance as evidenced by the morphological (severe wilting of leaves) phenotype (Figure 4a). PMC7540533.p029.s008: This is in accordance with the phenotype observed in the tomato null mutant of notabilis that is deficient in ABA synthesis (Burbidge et al.,; Thompson et al.,). PMC7540533.p029.s009: Reduced thermotolerance was also reported in Arabidopsis mutants with impaired NCED6 and NCED9 expression (Gabriele et al.,). PMC7540533.p029.s010: It is known that high ABA levels cause ROS outburst. PMC7540533.p029.s011: However, the leaves of Notabilis‐silenced plants show slightly increased ROS levels and HS‐induced cell death in comparison with vector control plants (Figure 4b). PMC7540533.p029.s012: This apparent anomaly may be attributed to a balance between low ROS levels due to reduced ABA levels and other pathways contributing to ROS production in response to HS. PMC7540533.p029.s013: The gas exchange analyses further supported the important role of Notabilis in thermotolerance. events.event_label: ASAT gene improves thermotolerance and seedling performance
events.evidence_context_text: PMC7540533.p026.s009: These parameters are routinely adopted to assess thermotolerance of plants. PMC7540533.p026.s010: The suppression of ASAT gene shows enhanced hydrogen peroxide (one of several reactive oxygen species) levels as well as cell death under HS as judged by DAB staining and Trypan blue staining, respectively (Figure 4b). PMC7540533.p026.s011: Gas exchange parameters measured by LI‐COR 6400 portable photosynthesis measuring system at the end of the heat treatment of ASAT‐silenced plants showed significant drop in net photosynthesis rate and water‐use efficiency (WUEi) as well as enhanced stomatal conductance and transpiration rate (Figure 4c–f) highlighting the role of ASAT as a positive regulator of thermotolerance. PMC7540533.p026.s012: This conclusion was further strengthened by assessing seedling performance under HS when ASAT gene was transiently overexpressed. PMC7540533.p026.s013: The seedling survival increased by nearly 17% in comparison with vector control (Figure 4g), and the reduction in hypocotyl length under HS was also around 8% in overexpression plants in comparison with 18% in vector control plants (Figure 4h).

events.event_label: ASAT catalyzes acylsugar biosynthesis
events.evidence_context_text: PMC7540533.p026.s001: Solyc09g014280 gene codes for an HXXXD‐type acyltransferase and belongs to the acylsugar acyltransferases (ASATs) class of the large and diverse BAHD family of acyltransferases (Moghe et al.,). PMC7540533.p026.s002: Acylsugars are a group of small specialized metabolites having diverse structures that are restricted to plants in the Solanaceae family that act as natural chemicals against pests like whiteflies and spider mites (Alba et al.,; Liedl et al.,). PMC7540533.p026.s003: Acylsugars are produced in the tip cell of the long glandular secreting trichomes using ASAT enzymes that catalyse sequential addition of specific acyl chains to the sucrose molecule using acyl CoA donors (Maldonado et al.,; Schilmiller et al.,). PMC7540533.p026.s004: Solyc09g014280 and Solyc05g052670 (another tolerant‐up gene but not being functionally characterized; Figure S10) are reported to be enriched in the glandular trichomes in tomato (Moghe et al.,).

events.event_label: PI‑II knock‑down enhances thermotolerance traits and participates in retrograde signalling
events.evidence_context_text: PMC7540533.p031.s001: Furthermore, another gene but a ‘sensitive‐up gene’ which is Solyc03g020030 was functionally characterized. PMC7540533.p031.s002: This belongs to the proteinase inhibitor‐II (PI‐II) serine‐PI family having dimeric domains in the protein, inhibiting trypsin and chymotrypsin (Tamhane et al.,). PMC7540533.p031.s003: The PI‐IIs are well documented in plant defence against insects, bacteria and fungi (Rehman et al.,). PMC7540533.p031.s004: Few reports in abiotic stress tolerance like drought, salinity, osmotic variations and pH are also documented (Huang et al.,; Shan et al.,; Srinivasan et al.,). PMC7540533.p031.s009: Moreover, there was appreciable difference phenotypically when VIGS was repeated in sensitive background (Pusa Ruby) (Figure 5b). PMC7540533.p031.s010: The CLN PI‐II knock‐down plants exhibited significant enhancement in WUEi and net photosynthetic rates as compared to TRV‐control plants under HS (Figure 5c). PMC7540533.p031.s011: In addition, both the transpiration rate and the stomatal conductance were lower in TRV‐PI‐II plants under HS (Figure 5c,d). PMC7540533.p031.s012: The PI‐II overexpression in CLN decreased the tolerance of the seedlings of this robust cultivar as evident from reduced hypocotyl length as well as survival rate under HS in comparison with vector control seedlings. PMC7540533.p031.s013: In contrast, VIGS‐based silencing of PI‐II in Pusa Ruby exhibited higher survival rate (Figure 5e–g). PMC7540533.p031.s015: In tomato, SlbZIP1 TF (Solyc01g079480.3) transcriptionally regulates PI‐II (Zhu et al.,). PMC7540533.p031.s016: When we checked our NGS data for a possible cultivar‐specific differential response of PI‐II by SlbZIP1 in response to HS, the TF was not significantly differentially regulated in CLN (1.85‐fold change) and CA4 (1.25‐fold change) (Table S2). PMC7540533.p031.s017: This suggested that HS‐mediated regulation of PI‐II is governed by other TFs or may have epigenetic regulation. PMC7540533.p031.s018: The down‐regulation of PI‐II by VIGS established its role in retrograde signalling in the regulation of HS. events.event_label: Heat stress up‑regulates HSF family members and several ERFs
events.evidence_context_text: PMC7540533.p022.s001: The most studied and well‐established TF family in response to HS is the HSF family. PMC7540533.p022.s002: Seven tomato HSFs (A1b, A2, A3, A4c, A5, A7 and C1) exhibited HS‐mediated up‐regulation in both the cultivars with highest up‐regulation for HSFA2 (Table S4). PMC7540533.p022.s003: We noted that the repressor class B HSF members, namely HSFB1, B2a and B2b, are up‐regulated only in the sensitive CA4. PMC7540533.p022.s004: Whether this has any direct correlation with CA4 being HS sensitive needs to be further evaluated. PMC7540533.p022.s009: Furthermore, we investigated the ERF family that showed highest number of HS‐responsive TFs and include AP2, DREBs and ERFs (Sakuma et al.,). PMC7540533.p022.s010: Various ERFs are reported to mediate development and stress response, but only few address their regulatory role in thermotolerance (Licausi et al.,; Klay et al.,; Müller and Munné‐Bosch, ). PMC7540533.p022.s011: Out of 147 ERFs reported in tomato, 30 members followed differential HS response in CLN and CA4 (Table S4). PMC7540533.p022.s012: Of these, 12 ERFs were similarly regulated (9 up‐regulated and 3‐regulated down) in both cultivars, while others were regulated in cultivar‐biased manner. PMC7540533.p022.s013: We selected three previously uncharacterized ERFs (ERF.F4, ERF.F5, and ERF.C1) in HS response based on the observation that they all maintained high levels in CLN and low levels in CA4 under HS. PMC7540533.p022.s014: This was validated by qRT‐PCR also (Figure 3e). PMC7540533.p022.s015: These 3 ERFs are reported in fruit development (Hao et al.,; Di Matteo et al.,) in tomato, but their role in thermotolerance was lacking. PMC7540533.p022.s016: Functional characterization of the above three ERFs by transient overexpression significantly enhanced (more than doubled) the tomato seedling survival rate under HS as compared to vector control plants (Figure 3f) with highest survival percentage for ERF.F5.

events.event_label: Heat stress up‑regulates multiple transcription‑factor families
events.evidence_context_text: PMC7540533.p020.s009: Of these, 3 TFs, viz. PMC7540533.p020.s010: HSFA2 (Solyc08g062960), and dehydration‐responsive element‐binding (DREB) TF (Solyc05g052410) were more than 100‐fold up‐regulated in both cultivars. PMC7540533.p020.s011: The plot of above 30 TFs clearly revealed that the heat‐tolerant CLN showed higher magnitude of HS‐mediated induction in majority of the TFs and common key TF pool was activated upon HS in both cultivars (Figure 3b). PMC7540533.p020.s012: Moreover, 26 TFs showed HS‐mediated strong repression (FC ≤ 5) in both cultivars (Figure 3c). PMC7540533.p020.s013: While Solyc08g008280 (WRKY‐53) was the top repressed candidate in CLN, Solyc10g005080 (late elongated hypocotyl) was highly down‐regulated TF in CA4 under HS (Figure 3c). PMC7540533.p020.s014: In addition, other TFs exhibiting strong repression belong to TF families bHLH (4), bZIP (2), C2H2 (3), MYB (3), TCP (2), WRKY (2) and YABBY (2) (Figure 3c and Table S4). PMC7540533.p020.s015: The highly HS‐responsive TF families with more than 20 of its members significantly modulated (up‐regulated and down‐regulated) at transcript level include bHLH, bZIP, C2H2, C3H, ERF, GRAS, HD‐ZIP, HSF, MYB, NAC and WRKY (Figure 3d). PMC7540533.p020.s016: Further, we scanned for those TF families that had at least two differentially regulated members in response to HS and also had ≥80% of its members exhibiting either up‐ or down‐regulation.

events.event_label: Notabilis contributes to thermotolerance via ABA biosynthesis pathway
events.evidence_context_text: PMC7540533.p029.s001: We then functionally validated the other tolerant‐up gene, Solyc07g056570 or Notabilis (9‐cis‐epoxycarotenoid dioxygenase 1). PMC7540533.p029.s002: Notabilis regulates the rate‐limiting step in the ABA biosynthesis pathway and has important role in water stress tolerance as evidenced by several reports (Burbidge et al.,; He et al.,; Luchi et al.,; Qin and Zeevaart,; Wan and Li,). PMC7540533.p029.s003: In Arabidopsis, ABA‐deficient and signalling mutants are involved in acquired thermotolerance acquisition (Larkindale and Huang,). PMC7540533.p029.s004: Arabidopsis thaliana NGATHA1 induces ABA biosynthesis by activating Notabilis homolog NCED3 gene during dehydration stress (Sato et al.,). PMC7540533.p029.s012: This apparent anomaly may be attributed to a balance between low ROS levels due to reduced ABA levels and other pathways contributing to ROS production in response to HS. PMC7540533.p029.s013: The gas exchange analyses further supported the important role of Notabilis in thermotolerance. PMC7540533.p029.s014: There was a significant reduction in the photosynthetic rate and water‐use efficiency along with a significant rise in stomatal conductance and enhanced transpiration rate in silenced plants in contrast to vector control plants (Figure 4c–f). PMC7540533.p029.s015: Overexpression of wheat TaNCED gene in Arabidopsis enhanced tolerance to drought stress by reducing stomatal conductance and transpiration (Tong et al.,). events.event_label: HSFA1s activates HSFA2 and HSFA2 participates in heat response pathways
events.evidence_context_text: PMC7540533.p005.s006: It is therefore necessary to have in‐depth understanding of the plant thermotolerance mechanisms at molecular level to achieve sustainable food production. PMC7540533.p005.s007: The canonical HSR involves HS sensing by Phytochrome B (Jung et al.,), the heat‐induced modulations at the level of membrane stability activating the calcium and lipid signalling (Saidi et al.,), and the interplay of conserved heat stress transcription factor‐heat shock protein (HSF‐HSP) module. PMC7540533.p005.s008: Upon HS, HSFA1s, the master regulators of HSR, are relieved from repression by HSP70/90, relocate from cytoplasm to nucleus and activate other HSFs (HSFA7s, HSFA2 and HSFBs), dehydration‐responsive element‐binding protein 2A (DREB2A) and multiprotein bridging factor 1C (MBF1C) (Liu et al.,; Mishra et al.,; Yoshida et al.,). PMC7540533.p005.s009: These transcriptional regulators further control the expression of several HS‐inducible genes including HSFA3, nuclear factor‐Y (NF‐Y) and HSPs (Chen et al.,; Sato et al.,; Schramm et al.,; Yoshida et al.,). PMC7540533.p005.s010: In Arabidopsis, HSFA1 regulates HSFBs that in turn finely regulate the HSR by repressing the activity of HSFA1s in a negative feedback loop (Ikeda et al.,). PMC7540533.p005.s012: Several HSF‐target modules like HSFA4a‐APX1 and HSFA5‐HSPs act independent of HSFA1 pathway (Liu et al.,; Von Koskull‐Doring et al.,). PMC7540533.p005.s013: In addition, a flexible mode of heat response pathways involving bZIP28‐, HSFA2‐ and ROS‐dependent signals is also demonstrated in Arabidopsis (Kataoka et al.,). PMC7540533.p005.s014: Apart from these canonical HSR circuits, PIF4 regulatory and associated signalling networks control plant thermo‐morphogenesis (Koini et al.,; Oh et al.,; Proveniers and Van Zanten,; Stavang et al.,). PMC7540533.p005.s015: The significance of post‐translational regulation in HS response is also well documented in plant HSR (Agarwal et al.,; Hahn et al.,; Miller et al.,; Mizoi et al.,; Sato et al.,; Vainonen et al.,). events.event_label: HSFA1s activates HSFA7s and MBF1C
events.evidence_context_text: PMC7540533.p005.s006: It is therefore necessary to have in‐depth understanding of the plant thermotolerance mechanisms at molecular level to achieve sustainable food production. PMC7540533.p005.s007: The canonical HSR involves HS sensing by Phytochrome B (Jung et al.,), the heat‐induced modulations at the level of membrane stability activating the calcium and lipid signalling (Saidi et al.,), and the interplay of conserved heat stress transcription factor‐heat shock protein (HSF‐HSP) module. PMC7540533.p005.s008: Upon HS, HSFA1s, the master regulators of HSR, are relieved from repression by HSP70/90, relocate from cytoplasm to nucleus and activate other HSFs (HSFA7s, HSFA2 and HSFBs), dehydration‐responsive element‐binding protein 2A (DREB2A) and multiprotein bridging factor 1C (MBF1C) (Liu et al.,; Mishra et al.,; Yoshida et al.,). PMC7540533.p005.s009: These transcriptional regulators further control the expression of several HS‐inducible genes including HSFA3, nuclear factor‐Y (NF‐Y) and HSPs (Chen et al.,; Sato et al.,; Schramm et al.,; Yoshida et al.,). PMC7540533.p005.s010: In Arabidopsis, HSFA1 regulates HSFBs that in turn finely regulate the HSR by repressing the activity of HSFA1s in a negative feedback loop (Ikeda et al.,).

events.event_label: HS reduces_or_impairs yield
events.evidence_context_text: PMC7540533.p006.s001: Tomato (Solanum lycopersicum) is the second most cultivated vegetable in the world. PMC7540533.p006.s002: HS leads to reduced tomato yield by affecting its vegetative as well as reproductive development (Pressman et al.,; Sato et al.,). PMC7540533.p006.s003: Transcript profiling using cDNA‐AFLP (Bita et al.,) and microarray analysis (Frank et al.,) as well as proteomic analyses (Mazzeo et al.,) of microspores has shown active involvement of HSPs, ROS scavengers, hormones, amino acid metabolism and nitrogen assimilation in tomato HS response. events.event_label: HS affects seeded fruits
events.evidence_context_text: PMC7540533.p009.s015: CLN is reported to be tolerant to salt stress (Saeed et al.,) and TMV infection (https://avrdc.org). PMC7540533.p009.s016: CLN has been shown to have higher plant vigour and pollen viability as compared to sensitive CA4 (Sangu et al.,). PMC7540533.p009.s017: In addition, CLN is reported to maintain highest percentage of seeded fruits under HS as compared to several other cultivars (Comlekcioglu and Soylu,). PMC7540533.p009.s018: The CLN and CA4 pair has been used as donor parents for generating mapping populations towards HS (https://avrdc.org). PMC7540533.p009.s019: Thus, decoding the transcriptome of these two cultivars in HS response should provide valuable resource for future research to improve thermotolerance in tomato.

events.event_label: HS affects leaf
events.evidence_context_text: PMC7540533.p014.s001: We next examined only that fraction of transcriptome that was significantly modulated (FDR P‐value ≤ 0.05 and fold change ≥2 and ≤−2 for up‐ and down‐regulated genes, respectively) in leaf under HS as depicted by volcano plots (Figure S2d). PMC7540533.p014.s002: A total of 6954 differentially expressing genes (DEGs) were identified between both cultivars (Figure 2a). PMC7540533.p014.s003: This number is the highest number of DEGs so far in tomato and the first for leaf utilizing contrasting cultivars (Bita et al.,; Fragkostefanakis et al.,; Fragkostefanakis et al.,; Keller et al.,). PMC7540533.p014.s004: A slightly higher number of HS‐responsive genes were present in the HS‐tolerant cultivar (5239 vs. 5138 in CLN and CA4, respectively); moreover, the up‐regulated events were more in tolerant cultivar [2580 (CLN) and 2496 (CA4)], while almost equal number of down‐regulated genes were present in both cultivars [2659 (CLN) and 2642 (CA4)] (Figure 2a).

events.event_label: HS associates_with HS response
events.evidence_context_text: PMC7540533.p020.s003: Out of these, 402 TFs belonging to 46 families were found to be heat‐responsive in at least one cultivar. PMC7540533.p020.s005: Similar HS response was evident for 201 (100 down/101 up) TFs in both cultivars (Figure 3a). PMC7540533.p020.s006: Thirty‐seven TFs (29 and 16 in CLN and CA4, respectively) exhibited very strong heat induction (FC > 10). PMC7540533.p020.s007: Thirty TFs followed conserved high (FC > 5) up‐regulation in both the cultivars, while 26 followed high (FC < −5) down‐regulation in both the cultivars.

relations.subject: Acylsugar acyltransferase

evidence.text: Functional characterization of three antagonistic genes by overexpression and silencing established Solyc09g014280 (Acylsugar acyltransferase) and Solyc07g056570 (Notabilis) that are up‐regulated in tolerant cultivar, as positive regulators of HS tolerance and Solyc03g020030 (Pin‐II proteinase inhibitor), that are down‐regulated in CLN1621L, as negative regulator of thermotolerance.

relations.evidence_context_text: PMC7540533.p003.s003: Comparative transcriptome‐wide signatures of a tolerant (CLN1621L) and sensitive (CA4) cultivar pair shortlisted from a pool of varieties exhibiting variable thermo‐sensitivity using physiological‐, survival‐ and yield‐related traits revealed redundant to cultivar‐specific HS regulation. PMC7540533.p003.s004: The antagonistically expressing genes encode enzymes and proteins that have roles in plant defence and abiotic stresses. PMC7540533.p003.s005: Functional characterization of three antagonistic genes by overexpression and silencing established Solyc09g014280 (Acylsugar acyltransferase) and Solyc07g056570 (Notabilis) that are up‐regulated in tolerant cultivar, as positive regulators of HS tolerance and Solyc03g020030 (Pin‐II proteinase inhibitor), that are down‐regulated in CLN1621L, as negative regulator of thermotolerance. PMC7540533.p003.s006: Transcriptional assessment of promoters of these genes by SNPs in stress‐responsive cis‐elements and promoter swapping experiments in opposite cultivar background showed inherent cultivar‐specific orchestration of transcription factors in regulating transcription. PMC7540533.p003.s007: Moreover, overexpression of three ethylene response transcription factors (ERF.C1/F4/F5) also improved HS tolerance in tomato.

relations.subject: High temperature

evidence.text: High temperature affects the phenological development, photosynthesis, respiration, initiation, expansion and senescence of plant organs (Wang et al.,).

relations.evidence_context_text: PMC7540533.p005.s001: Global warming is exposing plants to unfavourable temperature fluctuations resulting in detrimental effects on crop productivity and an ultimate threat to biodiversity and food security (Battisti and Naylor,; Challinor et al.,). relations.subject: Phytochrome B

evidence.text: The canonical HSR involves HS sensing by Phytochrome B (Jung et al.,), the heat‐induced modulations at the level of membrane stability activating the calcium and lipid signalling (Saidi et al.,), and the interplay of conserved heat stress transcription factor‐heat shock protein (HSF‐HSP) module.

relations.evidence_preview: The canonical HSR involves HS sensing by Phytochrome B (Jung et al.,), the heat‐induced modulations at the level of membrane stability activating the calcium and lipid signalling (Saidi et al.,), and the interplay of conserved heat stress transcription factor‐heat shock protein (HSF‐HSP) module.
relations.evidence_context_text: PMC7540533.p005.s006: It is therefore necessary to have in‐depth understanding of the plant thermotolerance mechanisms at molecular level to achieve sustainable food production. PMC7540533.p005.s007: The canonical HSR involves HS sensing by Phytochrome B (Jung et al.,), the heat‐induced modulations at the level of membrane stability activating the calcium and lipid signalling (Saidi et al.,), and the interplay of conserved heat stress transcription factor‐heat shock protein (HSF‐HSP) module. PMC7540533.p005.s008: Upon HS, HSFA1s, the master regulators of HSR, are relieved from repression by HSP70/90, relocate from cytoplasm to nucleus and activate other HSFs (HSFA7s, HSFA2 and HSFBs), dehydration‐responsive element‐binding protein 2A (DREB2A) and multiprotein bridging factor 1C (MBF1C) (Liu et al.,; Mishra et al.,; Yoshida et al.,). PMC7540533.p005.s009: These transcriptional regulators further control the expression of several HS‐inducible genes including HSFA3, nuclear factor‐Y (NF‐Y) and HSPs (Chen et al.,; Sato et al.,; Schramm et al.,; Yoshida et al.,). PMC7540533.p005.s010: In Arabidopsis, HSFA1 regulates HSFBs that in turn finely regulate the HSR by repressing the activity of HSFA1s in a negative feedback loop (Ikeda et al.,).

evidence_context_sentences.text: As sessile life forms, it is imperative for plants to evolve mechanisms to adapt to the thermal stress to minimize damage on their growth and reproduction.
This requires dynamic reprogramming at the level of transcriptome, proteome, metabolome, lipidome and epigenome leading to the activation of complex heat stress response (HSR) (Mittler et al.,).
It is therefore necessary to have in‐depth understanding of the plant thermotolerance mechanisms at molecular level to achieve sustainable food production.

The canonical HSR involves HS sensing by Phytochrome B (Jung et al.,), the heat‐induced modulations at the level of membrane stability activating the calcium and lipid signalling (Saidi et al.,), and the interplay of conserved heat stress transcription factor‐heat shock protein (HSF‐HSP) module.

Upon HS, HSFA1s, the master regulators of HSR, are relieved from repression by HSP70/90, relocate from cytoplasm to nucleus and activate other HSFs (HSFA7s, HSFA2 and HSFBs), dehydration‐responsive element‐binding protein 2A (DREB2A) and multiprotein bridging factor 1C (MBF1C) (Liu et al.,; Mishra et al.,; Yoshida et al.,).

evidence_context_sentences.text: As sessile life forms, it is imperative for plants to evolve mechanisms to adapt to the thermal stress to minimize damage on their growth and reproduction.
This requires dynamic reprogramming at the level of transcriptome, proteome, metabolome, lipidome and epigenome leading to the activation of complex heat stress response (HSR) (Mittler et al.,).
It is therefore necessary to have in‐depth understanding of the plant thermotolerance mechanisms at molecular level to achieve sustainable food production.

The canonical HSR involves HS sensing by Phytochrome B (Jung et al.,), the heat‐induced modulations at the level of membrane stability activating the calcium and lipid signalling (Saidi et al.,), and the interplay of conserved heat stress transcription factor‐heat shock protein (HSF‐HSP) module.

Upon HS, HSFA1s, the master regulators of HSR, are relieved from repression by HSP70/90, relocate from cytoplasm to nucleus and activate other HSFs (HSFA7s, HSFA2 and HSFBs), dehydration‐responsive element‐binding protein 2A (DREB2A) and multiprotein bridging factor 1C (MBF1C) (Liu et al.,; Mishra et al.,; Yoshida et al.,).

evidence_context_sentences.text: The canonical HSR involves HS sensing by Phytochrome B (Jung et al.,), the heat‐induced modulations at the level of membrane stability activating the calcium and lipid signalling (Saidi et al.,), and the interplay of conserved heat stress transcription factor‐heat shock protein (HSF‐HSP) module.

These transcriptional regulators further control the expression of several HS‐inducible genes including HSFA3, nuclear factor‐Y (NF‐Y) and HSPs (Chen et al.,; Sato et al.,; Schramm et al.,; Yoshida et al.,).
In Arabidopsis, HSFA1 regulates HSFBs that in turn finely regulate the HSR by repressing the activity of HSFA1s in a negative feedback loop (Ikeda et al.,).
Another critical regulator of thermotolerance is the C‐REPEAT BINDING FACTOR2 (RCF2)‐regulated NAC019 that acts as an upstream regulator of HSFA1b, HSFA6b, HSFA7a and HSFC1 (Guan et al.,).

evidence_context_sentences.text: In Arabidopsis, HSFA1 regulates HSFBs that in turn finely regulate the HSR by repressing the activity of HSFA1s in a negative feedback loop (Ikeda et al.,).
Another critical regulator of thermotolerance is the C‐REPEAT BINDING FACTOR2 (RCF2)‐regulated NAC019 that acts as an upstream regulator of HSFA1b, HSFA6b, HSFA7a and HSFC1 (Guan et al.,).
Several HSF‐target modules like HSFA4a‐APX1 and HSFA5‐HSPs act independent of HSFA1 pathway (Liu et al.,; Von Koskull‐Doring et al.,).
In addition, a flexible mode of heat response pathways involving bZIP28‐, HSFA2‐ and ROS‐dependent signals is also demonstrated in Arabidopsis (Kataoka et al.,).

Apart from these canonical HSR circuits, PIF4 regulatory and associated signalling networks control plant thermo‐morphogenesis (Koini et al.,; Oh et al.,; Proveniers and Van Zanten,; Stavang et al.,).

relations.subject: HS

evidence.text: HS leads to reduced tomato yield by affecting its vegetative as well as reproductive development (Pressman et al.,; Sato et al.,).

relations.evidence_preview: HS leads to reduced tomato yield by affecting its vegetative as well as reproductive development (Pressman et al.,; Sato et al.,).
relations.evidence_context_text: PMC7540533.p006.s001: Tomato (Solanum lycopersicum) is the second most cultivated vegetable in the world. PMC7540533.p006.s002: HS leads to reduced tomato yield by affecting its vegetative as well as reproductive development (Pressman et al.,; Sato et al.,). PMC7540533.p006.s003: Transcript profiling using cDNA‐AFLP (Bita et al.,) and microarray analysis (Frank et al.,) as well as proteomic analyses (Mazzeo et al.,) of microspores has shown active involvement of HSPs, ROS scavengers, hormones, amino acid metabolism and nitrogen assimilation in tomato HS response. PMC7540533.p006.s004: Heat‐inducible tomato HSP21 protects PSII from the HS‐induced oxidative stress and also plays role in fruit ripening (Neta‐Sharir et al.,).

evidence_context_sentences.text: Tomato (Solanum lycopersicum) is the second most cultivated vegetable in the world.
HS leads to reduced tomato yield by affecting its vegetative as well as reproductive development (Pressman et al.,; Sato et al.,).

Transcript profiling using cDNA‐AFLP (Bita et al.,) and microarray analysis (Frank et al.,) as well as proteomic analyses (Mazzeo et al.,) of microspores has shown active involvement of HSPs, ROS scavengers, hormones, amino acid metabolism and nitrogen assimilation in tomato HS response.
Heat‐inducible tomato HSP21 protects PSII from the HS‐induced oxidative stress and also plays role in fruit ripening (Neta‐Sharir et al.,).

evidence.text: Solyc01g065530 (protein COBRA), Solyc01g081250 (glutathione s‐transferase), Solyc01g087020 (transmembrane protein), Solyc01g095140 (late embryogenesis abundant protein), Solyc01g107780 (glycosyltransferase), Solyc03g116890 (WRKY transcription factor 39), Soly05g052670, Solyc05g052680 and Solyc09g014280 (all three HXXXD‐type acyltransferase family proteins), Solyc06g049070 (nucleotide/sugar transporter family protein), Solyc07g008103 (blue copper protein), Solyc07g056570 (Notabilis), Solyc07g064410 (Kua‐ubiquitin conjugating enzyme hybrid), Solyc09g075920 (serine/threonine‐protein kinase), Solyc09g092500 (glycosyltransferase) and Solyc10g081570 (Marmande), were up‐regulated in CLN but down‐regulated in CA4 upon HS.

relations.evidence_context_text: PMC7540533.p014.s006: Surprisingly, only 21 genes showed an opposite (antagonistic) HS regulation between CLN and CA4 (Figure 2a,b). PMC7540533.p014.s007: Five genes, viz. Solyc03g020030 (PIN‐type‐II proteinase inhibitor 69), Solyc09g084450 (proteinase inhibitor I), Solyc12g013700 (Stem‐specific protein tsjt1), Solyc01g079530 (RING/FYVE/PHD zinc finger superfamily protein) and Solyc12g042500 (gibberellin‐regulated family protein), were down‐regulated in CLN but were up‐regulated in CA4. PMC7540533.p014.s008: There were 16 genes, viz. PMC7540533.p014.s009: Solyc01g065530 (protein COBRA), Solyc01g081250 (glutathione s‐transferase), Solyc01g087020 (transmembrane protein), Solyc01g095140 (late embryogenesis abundant protein), Solyc01g107780 (glycosyltransferase), Solyc03g116890 (WRKY transcription factor 39), Soly05g052670, Solyc05g052680 and Solyc09g014280 (all three HXXXD‐type acyltransferase family proteins), Solyc06g049070 (nucleotide/sugar transporter family protein), Solyc07g008103 (blue copper protein), Solyc07g056570 (Notabilis), Solyc07g064410 (Kua‐ubiquitin conjugating enzyme hybrid), Solyc09g075920 (serine/threonine‐protein kinase), Solyc09g092500 (glycosyltransferase) and Solyc10g081570 (Marmande), were up‐regulated in CLN but down‐regulated in CA4 upon HS. PMC7540533.p014.s010: Gene ontology (GO) enrichment of these antagonistic genes highlighted the significant enrichment of negative regulation of endopeptidase, peptidase and proteolysis, response to water, pollination and transferase activity (Figure 2c).

evidence_context_sentences.text: A high magnitude of up‐regulation has also been reported in anthers of heat‐tolerant (Heat Set 1) tomato genotype in comparison with sensitive genotype, Falcorosso (Bita et al.,).

Solyc03g020030 (PIN‐type‐II proteinase inhibitor 69), Solyc09g084450 (proteinase inhibitor I), Solyc12g013700 (Stem‐specific protein tsjt1), Solyc01g079530 (RING/FYVE/PHD zinc finger superfamily protein) and Solyc12g042500 (gibberellin‐regulated family protein), were down‐regulated in CLN but were up‐regulated in CA4.
There were 16 genes, viz.

relations.subject: HS

evidence.text: Around ~49% (3402 out of 6954) HS‐responsive genes followed conserved HS‐regulated gene expression; 1752 DEGs were up‐regulated and 1650 DEGs were down‐regulated in both cultivars (Figure 2a), signifying a core universal HS response.

relations.evidence_preview: Around ~49% (3402 out of 6954) HS‐responsive genes followed conserved HS‐regulated gene expression; 1752 DEGs were up‐regulated and 1650 DEGs were down‐regulated in both cultivars (Figure 2a), signifying a core universal HS response.
relations.evidence_context_text: PMC7540533.p016.s001: Around ~49% (3402 out of 6954) HS‐responsive genes followed conserved HS‐regulated gene expression; 1752 DEGs were up‐regulated and 1650 DEGs were down‐regulated in both cultivars (Figure 2a), signifying a core universal HS response. PMC7540533.p016.s002: GO enrichment analysis suggested the involvement of up‐regulated genes in cellular response to nutrient starvation specifically phosphate starvation in addition to protein folding (Figure S3a,b). PMC7540533.p016.s003: Furthermore, proteins belonging to chaperones, winged helix TFs, mRNA splicing and processing factors were significantly enriched among the conserved up‐regulated genes (Figure S3c). PMC7540533.p016.s004: KEGG pathways like protein processing in ER, spliceosome and ubiquitin‐mediated proteolysis were enriched in conserved up‐regulated genes (Table S3).

evidence_context_sentences.text: Around ~49% (3402 out of 6954) HS‐responsive genes followed conserved HS‐regulated gene expression; 1752 DEGs were up‐regulated and 1650 DEGs were down‐regulated in both cultivars (Figure 2a), signifying a core universal HS response.
GO enrichment analysis suggested the involvement of up‐regulated genes in cellular response to nutrient starvation specifically phosphate starvation in addition to protein folding (Figure S3a,b).
Furthermore, proteins belonging to chaperones, winged helix TFs, mRNA splicing and processing factors were significantly enriched among the conserved up‐regulated genes (Figure S3c).
KEGG pathways like protein processing in ER, spliceosome and ubiquitin‐mediated proteolysis were enriched in conserved up‐regulated genes (Table S3).

relations.subject: HS

evidence.text: Genes exhibiting the CLN‐specific down‐regulation were enriched in terms associated with ‘photosynthesis’ in biological process and cellular component categories, while glycopeptide alpha‐N‐acetylgalactosaminidase activity, cysteine synthase activity, chlorophyll binding, and ATPase activity were enriched among the molecular function category (Figure S7).

relations.evidence_preview: Genes exhibiting the CLN‐specific down‐regulation were enriched in terms associated with ‘photosynthesis’ in biological process and cellular component categories, while glycopeptide alpha‐N‐acetylgalactosaminidase activity, cysteine synthase activity, chlorophyll binding, and ATPase activity were enriched among the molecular function category (Figure S7).
relations.evidence_context_text: PMC7540533.p017.s003: To further discriminate the true cultivar‐biased HS‐responsive DEGs, the above genes were filtered out by adopting strict parameters (See Materials and Methods). PMC7540533.p017.s004: As a result, 218 up‐ and 239 down‐regulated genes showed CA4‐specific HS regulation, while 220 up‐ and 213 down‐regulated genes followed CLN‐specific HS response (Figure 2a; Table S2). PMC7540533.p017.s005: Genes exhibiting the CLN‐specific down‐regulation were enriched in terms associated with ‘photosynthesis’ in biological process and cellular component categories, while glycopeptide alpha‐N‐acetylgalactosaminidase activity, cysteine synthase activity, chlorophyll binding, and ATPase activity were enriched among the molecular function category (Figure S7). PMC7540533.p017.s006: For genes exhibiting the CLN‐specific up‐regulation, there was no significant enrichment, while there was significant enrichment of translation, ribosomal proteins, ribosome, structural constituents of ribosomes, etc., in CA4‐specific up‐regulated genes (Figure S8). PMC7540533.p017.s007: Role of calcium‐mediated signalling and GTPase activity was highlighted among the CA4‐specific down‐regulated genes (Figure S9).

evidence_context_sentences.text: To further discriminate the true cultivar‐biased HS‐responsive DEGs, the above genes were filtered out by adopting strict parameters (See Materials and Methods).
As a result, 218 up‐ and 239 down‐regulated genes showed CA4‐specific HS regulation, while 220 up‐ and 213 down‐regulated genes followed CLN‐specific HS response (Figure 2a; Table S2).

Genes exhibiting the CLN‐specific down‐regulation were enriched in terms associated with ‘photosynthesis’ in biological process and cellular component categories, while glycopeptide alpha‐N‐acetylgalactosaminidase activity, cysteine synthase activity, chlorophyll binding, and ATPase activity were enriched among the molecular function category (Figure S7).

For genes exhibiting the CLN‐specific up‐regulation, there was no significant enrichment, while there was significant enrichment of translation, ribosomal proteins, ribosome, structural constituents of ribosomes, etc., in CA4‐specific up‐regulated genes (Figure S8).
Role of calcium‐mediated signalling and GTPase activity was highlighted among the CA4‐specific down‐regulated genes (Figure S9).

evidence_context_sentences.text: To further discriminate the true cultivar‐biased HS‐responsive DEGs, the above genes were filtered out by adopting strict parameters (See Materials and Methods).
As a result, 218 up‐ and 239 down‐regulated genes showed CA4‐specific HS regulation, while 220 up‐ and 213 down‐regulated genes followed CLN‐specific HS response (Figure 2a; Table S2).

Genes exhibiting the CLN‐specific down‐regulation were enriched in terms associated with ‘photosynthesis’ in biological process and cellular component categories, while glycopeptide alpha‐N‐acetylgalactosaminidase activity, cysteine synthase activity, chlorophyll binding, and ATPase activity were enriched among the molecular function category (Figure S7).

For genes exhibiting the CLN‐specific up‐regulation, there was no significant enrichment, while there was significant enrichment of translation, ribosomal proteins, ribosome, structural constituents of ribosomes, etc., in CA4‐specific up‐regulated genes (Figure S8).
Role of calcium‐mediated signalling and GTPase activity was highlighted among the CA4‐specific down‐regulated genes (Figure S9).

evidence_context_sentences.text: Out of these, 402 TFs belonging to 46 families were found to be heat‐responsive in at least one cultivar.
In CLN and CA4, 314 (152 down/162 up) and 285 (152 down/133 up) TFs were significantly differentially regulated, respectively (Figure 3a, Table S4).
Similar HS response was evident for 201 (100 down/101 up) TFs in both cultivars (Figure 3a).
Thirty‐seven TFs (29 and 16 in CLN and CA4, respectively) exhibited very strong heat induction (FC > 10).
Thirty TFs followed conserved high (FC > 5) up‐regulation in both the cultivars, while 26 followed high (FC < −5) down‐regulation in both the cultivars.

PMC7540533.p020.s014: In addition, other TFs exhibiting strong repression belong to TF families bHLH (4), bZIP (2), C2H2 (3), MYB (3), TCP (2), WRKY (2) and YABBY (2) (Figure 3c and Table S4). evidence_context_sentences.text: Thirty‐seven TFs (29 and 16 in CLN and CA4, respectively) exhibited very strong heat induction (FC > 10).
Thirty TFs followed conserved high (FC > 5) up‐regulation in both the cultivars, while 26 followed high (FC < −5) down‐regulation in both the cultivars.

These highly transcribed TFs include members belonging to ERF (5 members), CO‐like (3 members), HSFs (4 TFs); 4 members each of MYB and C3H, C2C2‐CO‐like and NAC (2); and 1 each of B3, bHLH, bZIP, C2H2, Dof1, FAR1, HD‐ZIP1 and NF‐YB D (Figure 3b).
Of these, 3 TFs, viz.
HSFA2 (Solyc08g062960), and dehydration‐responsive element‐binding (DREB) TF (Solyc05g052410) were more than 100‐fold up‐regulated in both cultivars.
